content.full_text:
California 
Fair Political 
Practices Commission 
July 18, 1988 
Nancy Leonard 
Assistant to the City Manager/ 
Employee Relations 
5275 Orange Avenue 
Cypress, CA 
90630 
Dear Ms. Leonard: 
Re: 
Your Request for Advice 
Our File No. A-88-271 
We have received your letter concerning the new 
restrictions on publicly-funded mass mailings under Proposition 
73. 
Enclosed are copies of our first interim advice letters 
interpreting the mass mailing restrictions. 
After examining 
the new statute and the analysis by the Legislative Analyst, we 
have concluded that the voters intended to restrict mailings by 
state and local elected officials, but did not intend to ban 
publicly-funded mailings entirely. 
Based on these interim 
advice letters, the sample newsletter you provided with your 
letter would be permitted, since it contains no reference to 
elected officials except in the standard letterhead or logotype. 
In the future, this may change. 
We expect to address 
additional questions concerning the mass mailing restrictions 
in Proposition 73, and we will include your name on our mailing 
list for information on the subject. 
DMG:KED:plh 
Enclosures 
Sincerely, 
Diane M. Griffiths 
General Counsel 
f< 
By: 
, 
Kathryn E. Donovan 
Counsel, Legal Division 
428 J Street, Suite 800 • P.O. Box 807 • Sacramento CA 95804-0807 • (916) 322-5660

sections.question_synthetic: Whether a city newsletter containing standard letterhead/logotype references to elected officials complies with Proposition 73's restrictions on publicly-funded mass mailings.
sections.conclusion_synthetic: The sample newsletter provided is permitted under Proposition 73 because it contains no reference to elected officials except in standard letterhead or logotype. The voters intended to restrict mailings by elected officials but not ban publicly-funded mailings entirely.
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: No formal QUESTION or CONCLUSION sections present; advice embedded in letter body. Response to incoming request regarding Proposition 73 compliance. Interim guidance letter with indication that interpretation may evolve.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: Whether a city newsletter containing standard letterhead/logotype references to elected officials complies with Proposition 73's restrictions on publicly-funded mass mailings.

CONCLUSION: The sample newsletter provided is permitted under Proposition 73 because it contains no reference to elected officials except in standard letterhead or logotype. The voters intended to restrict mailings by elected officials but not ban publicly-funded mailings entirely.

Enclosed are copies of our first interim advice letters interpreting the mass mailing restrictions. After examining the new statute and the analysis by the Legislative Analyst, we have concluded that the voters intended to restrict mailings by state and local elected officials, but did not intend to ban publicly-funded mailings entirely. Based on these interim advice letters, the sample newsletter you provided with your letter would be permitted, since it contains no reference to elected officials except in the standard letterhead or logotype. In the future, this may change. embedding.summary: FPPC advises that Cypress, California's sample newsletter complies with Proposition 73's mass mailing restrictions because it lacks substantive references to elected officials beyond standard letterhead. The Commission concluded that Proposition 73 restricts but does not entirely ban publicly-funded mailings.
